subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you will explore three closely linked ideas: measurement of length, perimeter, and area. You will learn how to measure how long something is using the right units, how to find the distance all the way around a shape, and how to work out how much flat space a shape covers. These three skills belong together, and once you understand them you will start to see them everywhere in daily life.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself with a pencil and paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Keep a ruler, a pencil, and some paper beside you as you read. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by looking – you must do it. Measure real objects, draw the shapes, count the squares, and say the answers out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Let us begin exploring the world of measurement together.
Choose the best unit to measure a length: millimetres, centimetres, metres or kilometres.
Measure objects accurately using a ruler, lining up the zero mark correctly.
Convert between units in the metric system (mm ↔ cm ↔ m ↔ km).
Estimate a length sensibly and then measure to check.
Find the perimeter of rectangles, squares, triangles and other polygons.
Find the area of rectangles and squares using length × width.
Find area by counting squares on grid paper.
Explain the difference between perimeter and area, and use the correct units for each.

concepts.title: Length and Choosing the Right Unit
concepts.explanation: Length is the measurement of how long something is from one end to the other. We measure length so often that we have several different units to choose from, depending on how big or small the thing is. Using the right unit is a bit like choosing the right container: you would not carry water in a spoon, and you would not fill a spoon from a bucket. In the metric system – the system used almost everywhere in the world – the units of length from smallest to largest are:
concepts.example: Millimetre (mm) – for very small lengths, like the thickness of a coin or the width of a fingernail.

concepts.title: Measuring with a Ruler
concepts.explanation: A ruler is a tool for measuring length. Most rulers show centimetres, with each centimetre divided into ten small parts – those small parts are millimetres. Measuring well is a skill, and doing it carefully makes all the difference between a right answer and a wrong one. Follow these three steps every time you measure with a ruler:
concepts.example: Place the zero mark of the ruler exactly at one end of the object. This is the most important step – do not start at the edge of the ruler or at the number 1 by mistake.

concepts.title: Converting Between Units
concepts.explanation: Here are the key metric conversions to learn by heart: 10 millimetres (mm) = 1 centimetre (cm)
concepts.example: There is a simple pattern to converting. When you go from a larger unit to a smaller unit, the number gets bigger, so you multiply. When you go from a smaller unit to a larger unit, the number gets smaller, so you…

concepts.title: Estimating Length
concepts.explanation: An estimate is a sensible guess about a measurement made before you measure exactly. Estimating is useful when you do not need an exact answer, and it is also a way of checking – if your careful measurement is far from your estimate, that is a warning to measure again. Good mathematicians estimate first out of habit. To make a good estimate, think about the size of the unit and compare the object to something you already know. You know your finger is about 7 cm wide, so you can use it as a ruler in your mind. You…
concepts.example: Here are some useful lengths to keep in your memory as reference points: a pencil is about 15–20 cm long, a door is about 2 m tall, a new pencil eraser is about 4 cm long, and a doorway is about 1 m wide. When you carry…

concepts.title: Perimeter – the Distance Around
concepts.explanation: Perimeter is the distance all the way around the outside of a shape. Imagine an ant walking around the very edge of a shape until it returns to where it started – the total distance it walks is the perimeter. Because perimeter is a length, it is measured in units of length like millimetres, centimetres, or metres. It is never measured in square units. The most basic way to find a perimeter is simply to add up the lengths of all the sides. This works for any shape with straight sides. For example, if a rectangle…
concepts.example: For rectangles and squares there are helpful shortcuts, because their sides follow a rule. In a rectangle, the opposite sides are equal, so there are really only two different side lengths: the length and the width.…

concepts.title: Area – the Space Inside
concepts.explanation: Area is the amount of flat space inside a shape. If perimeter is the distance an ant walks around the edge, then area is the amount of floor the ant could cover with tiles inside the shape. Because area measures a flat surface, it is measured in square units, such as square centimetres (cm²) or square metres (m²). The best way to understand area is to think of covering the shape with unit squares. A square centimetre (cm²) is a little square that is exactly 1 cm long on each side. The area of a shape is simply the…
concepts.example: Imagine a rectangle that is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide, drawn on grid paper. Along the length you can fit 8 squares in a row. There are 5 such rows going down. So the total number of squares is 8 in each row, 5 rows, which…

methods.title: How to measure with a ruler
methods.example: Which unit – mm, cm, m, or km – is best for measuring the distance between two cities?
steps: Line up the zero mark with one end of the object.
Read the number at the other end of the object.
Look straight down to avoid a reading error.

methods.title: How to find a perimeter
methods.example: A ribbon is 2 m 35 cm long. How long is it in centimetres?
steps: Find the length of every side.
Add all the side lengths together (or use the rectangle or square formula).
Write the answer in a length unit such as cm or m.

methods.title: How to find an area
methods.example: A rectangular garden near Kismaayo has a length of 8 m and a width of 5 m. How much fencing is needed to go around it?
steps: Find the length and the width.
Multiply length by width.
Write the answer in square units such as cm² or m².

methods.title: How to convert units
methods.example: A square picture frame has sides of 15 cm each. What is the distance around the frame?
steps: Decide if you are going to a smaller or larger unit.
Smaller unit: multiply (×10, ×100 or ×1000).
Larger unit: divide (÷10, ÷100 or ÷1000).

methods.title: Area of a rectangle
methods.example: A rectangular carpet is 6 m long and 4 m wide. What is its area?
steps: Solution: Area asks how much flat space the carpet covers, so we multiply: Area = length × width = 6 × 4 = 24 m².
Do not forget the square units – area is always in square units.
Check the area of a rectangle result carefully.

methods.title: Area of a square
methods.example: A square tile has sides of 20 cm. What is the area of the tile?
steps: Solution: Area of a square = side × side = 20 × 20 = 400 cm².
Check the area of a square result carefully.
Check the area of a square result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right unit
workedExamples.prompt: Which unit – mm, cm, m, or km – is best for measuring the distance between two cities?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cities are very far apart, so we need the largest unit of length. The best unit is the kilometre (km). Millimetres or centimetres would give a huge, awkward number, and even metres would be in the thousands.

workedExamples.title: Convert metres to centimetres
workedExamples.prompt: A ribbon is 2 m 35 cm long. How long is it in centimetres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First change the metres into centimetres: 2 m = 2 × 100 = 200 cm. Then add the extra centimetres: 200 + 35 = 235 cm. When you go from a larger unit to a smaller unit, the number gets bigger, which matches what we found.

workedExamples.title: Perimeter of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular garden near Kismaayo has a length of 8 m and a width of 5 m. How much fencing is needed to go around it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Fencing goes around the edge, so we need the perimeter. Using the formula: Perimeter = 2 × (length + width) = 2 × (8 + 5) = 2 × 13 = 26 m of fencing. We could also add all four sides: 8 + 5 + 8 + 5 = 26 m, which gives the same answer.

workedExamples.title: Perimeter of a square
workedExamples.prompt: A square picture frame has sides of 15 cm each. What is the distance around the frame?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: All four sides of a square are equal, so Perimeter = 4 × side = 4 × 15 = 60 cm.

workedExamples.title: Area of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular carpet is 6 m long and 4 m wide. What is its area?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Area asks how much flat space the carpet covers, so we multiply: Area = length × width = 6 × 4 = 24 m². Do not forget the square units – area is always in square units.

workedExamples.title: Area of a square
workedExamples.prompt: A square tile has sides of 20 cm. What is the area of the tile?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Area of a square = side × side = 20 × 20 = 400 cm².

workedExamples.title: Find a missing side
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle has a perimeter of 30 cm. Its length is 8 cm. What is its width?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start from the formula: Perimeter = 2 × (length + width). So 30 = 2 × (8 + width). Divide both sides by 2: 15 = 8 + width. Take away 8: width = 15 − 8 = 7 cm. You can check: 2 × (8 + 7) = 2 × 15 = 30 cm. Correct!

workedExamples.title: Perimeter and area together
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular room is 6 m long and 4 m wide. How much baseboard is needed to go around it, and how many square tiles of 1 m² are needed to cover the floor?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Baseboard goes around the edge, so use perimeter: 2 × (6 + 4) = 2 × 10 = 20 m of baseboard. Tiles cover the floor, so use area: 6 × 4 = 24 m², which means 24 tiles. Notice how one question needed perimeter and the other needed area – always ask which you need.

workedExamples.title: Tiles to cover a floor
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular room is 6 m long and 5 m wide. Each square tile has an area of 1 m². How many tiles cover the floor?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the floor area: 6 × 5 = 30 m². Since each tile covers 1 m², you need 30 ÷ 1 = 30 tiles.

workedExamples.title: Same area, different perimeter
workedExamples.prompt: Find two different rectangles with whole-number sides that each have an area of 12 cm². Which has the smaller perimeter?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We need pairs of whole numbers that multiply to 12. One choice is 1 × 12, another is 3 × 4. Both give an area of 12 cm². Their perimeters differ: the 1 by 12 rectangle has perimeter 2 × (1 + 12) = 26 cm, while the 3 by 4 rectangle has perimeter 2 × (3 + 4) = 14 cm. So the 3 by 4 rectangle has the smaller perimeter. Shapes closer to a square use less perimeter for the same area.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: For a rectangle 9 m long and 5 m wide, find both the perimeter and the area.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find all the rectangles with whole-number sides that have a perimeter of 20 cm. List each length and width.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Perimeter 20 cm means length + width = 10: so 1×9, 2×8, 3×7, 4×6, and 5×5.

activities.title: Measurement Scavenger Hunt
activities.materials: a ruler or tape measure, paper, a pencil.
steps: Make a list of 10 objects around your home.
Estimate the length of each object first, and write your guess down.
Measure each object with your ruler or tape measure and write the real length.
Compare your estimates with the real measurements. Which were closest?
For each object, decide whether centimetres or metres is the better unit.

activities.title: Perimeter Walk
activities.materials: a tape measure or a length of string, paper, a pencil.
steps: Choose a rectangular object or space – a table, a mat, or even a room.
Measure each side and write down the length.
Add all the sides together to find the perimeter.
Check your answer using the rectangle formula: 2 × (length + width).
Do the same for three more objects and compare their perimeters.

activities.title: Area with Square Tiles
activities.materials: grid paper, or small square objects like biscuits, tiles, or paper squares.
steps: Draw a rectangle on grid paper, or lay out a rectangle using square objects.
Count the squares inside to find the area.
Now find the area using length × width and check that the two answers match.
Make three more rectangles and record the length, width, and area of each.
Challenge: Make a shape on grid paper that has an area of 16 squares but is not a rectangle. Count the squares to prove it.

activities.title: Same Perimeter, Different Area (Investigation)
activities.materials: grid paper, a ruler, a pencil.
steps: On grid paper, draw four different rectangles that all have a perimeter of 24 cm.
Estimate the area of each rectangle before counting.
Count the squares to find the exact area of each.
Record your results in a table with columns for length, width, perimeter, and area.
Which rectangle has the largest area? What do you notice about its shape?

activities.title: Design a Garden (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a family member to work with.
steps: Imagine you are planning a rectangular vegetable garden for your family near Hargeisa.
Choose a length and a width in metres and draw the garden.
Work out the perimeter to decide how much fencing wire is needed.
Work out the area to decide how much space there is for planting.
Talk with your family member about whether a long thin garden or a square one is better, and why.

activities.title: The Conversion Card Game
steps: On some cards write measurements in one unit, like 3 m, 40 mm, or 2 km.
On other cards write the matching measurement in a different unit, like 300 cm, 4 cm, or 2000 m.
Shuffle the cards and lay them face down.
Take turns turning over two cards and keep the pair if they are equal. The player with the most pairs wins.
This game makes converting between units quick and automatic.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: The rule for the area of a rectangle

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: For rectangles and squares there are helpful shortcuts, because their sides follow a rule. In a rectangle, the opposite sides are equal, so there are really only two different side lengths: the length and the width. Instead of adding four numbers, we can add the length and width and then double the answer:

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: Imagine a rectangle that is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide, drawn on grid paper. Along the length you can fit 8 squares in a row. There are 5 such rows going down. So the total number of squares is 8 in each row, 5 rows, which is 8 × 5 = 40 squares. This is exactly why the rule for the area of a rectangle is:

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: Say the rule out loud before you solve: “Perimeter is add the sides; area is length times width.”

terms: How long something is, end to end
The flat space inside a shape
A square used to measure area (cm², m²)
Change from one unit to another
A flat shape with straight sides
commonMistakes: Write the small ² for every area answer
Area = length × width, not length + width
Place 0, not 1, at the end of the object
Smaller unit = multiply; larger unit = divide

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself with a pencil and paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can keep a ruler, a pencil, and some paper beside you as you read. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by looking – you must do it. Measure real objects, draw the shapes, count the squares, and say the answers out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Let us begin exploring the world of measurement together.
I can choose the best unit to measure a length: millimetres, centimetres, metres or kilometres.
I can measure objects accurately using a ruler, lining up the zero mark correctly.
I can convert between units in the metric system (mm ↔ cm ↔ m ↔ km).
I can estimate a length sensibly and then measure to check.
I can find the perimeter of rectangles, squares, triangles and other polygons.
I can find the area of rectangles and squares using length × width.
